title: The First Entry into Egypt (Monthly) — St. Methodius of Olympus & St. Adauctus the Martyr
narrative:
title": "The First Entry into Egypt (Monthly) — St. Methodius of Olympus & St. Adauctus the Martyr

narrative": "title\": \"The First Entry into Egypt (Monthly) — St. Methodius of Olympus & St. Adauctus the Martyr

narrative\": \"The Monthly Commemoration of the First Entry of the Holy Family into Egypt, St. Methodius the Bishop of Olympus the Martyr and Theologian, and St. Adauctus the Martyr of Phrygia

On the twentieth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of the First Entry of Our Lord Jesus Christ into Egypt — the arrival of the Holy Family in the land of the Pharaohs, the fulfilment of the prophet Hosea's word 'Out of Egypt I called my son,' the moment when the infant God crossed the border from Palestine into Africa and the land that had been the place of Israel's slavery became the place of the Messiah's refuge. The theology of the Holy Family's flight into Egypt is one of the most distinctive and cherished elements of the Ethiopian liturgical tradition: Egypt is not merely a foreign land to which the Holy Family fled but a land whose ancient spiritual geography was prepared over centuries for this visitation, and the tradition of the Coptic and Ethiopian churches preserves in detail the route of the Holy Family through Egypt, the miracles they performed, the places they blessed, the idols that fell as they passed.

The monthly commemoration of the First Entry into Egypt in the Tewahedo calendar is the community's monthly renewal of its understanding that the faith it practises is not a religion imported from a distant and foreign culture but one whose sacred geography includes Egypt and Ethiopia as intimately as it includes Bethlehem and Jerusalem. The Christ-child who came to Egypt came to the same continent on which Ethiopia stands, and the tradition's reverence for the flight into Egypt is inseparable from its understanding of the deep African roots of the Christian faith: Egypt gave the world Christian monasticism, Christian theology, and the most ancient continuous Christian liturgical tradition outside of Palestine itself. The community that gathers monthly around the feast of the First Entry is gathering around one of the founding events of African Christianity, one of the moments in which God's presence in Africa was most visibly and literally enacted.

Saint Methodius of Olympus is one of the most significant and least-remembered of the early Christian theologians: a bishop of the late third and early fourth centuries whose works include the Symposium — a dialogue on virginity modelled formally on Plato's Symposium but theologically oriented toward the resurrection and the virginal life as anticipations of the heavenly existence — and who was among the first to develop a sustained theological response to Origen's more speculative positions on the pre-existence of souls and the nature of the resurrection body. Methodius is the tradition's pre-Nicene theologian of the resurrection: his insistence that the body raised at the last day is genuinely and substantially continuous with the body buried in the earth was a crucial contribution to the tradition's ongoing effort to specify what the resurrection actually means, and his martyrdom under Diocletian gives his theological work the seal of personal commitment that the tradition always values in its doctors.

Saint Adauctus the Martyr of Phrygia enters this feast as one of the tradition's most charming accidental martyrs: a pagan official in the imperial administration who was accompanying a group of Christians to their execution and who, moved by their composure and their witness as they walked to their deaths, spontaneously declared himself a Christian and joined them, adding himself to the number of those to be killed. The tradition records that the soldiers and officials were initially confused by this unexpected addition to their martyr-group, but Adauctus was firm, and he was executed alongside the Christians whose witness had converted him within minutes of that conversion's completion. His very name — Adauctus, 'the added one' — is the tradition's reflection on the paradox of his martyrdom: he was added to the number of the martyrs by an act of spontaneous faith so sudden that it barely had time to be anything other than grace, pure and unmediated and complete. The twentieth day of Sene thus gathers around the great feast of the Holy Family's arrival in Africa a theologian of the resurrection and a man who found his faith on the way to watching others die for theirs. Amen.
